head.hi: The Secretary of State to the Consul General at Shanghai (Cunningham)

div.p: 95. Your 100, February 23, 3 p.m., and Department’s 61, February 29, 5 p.m.

1. With further reference to the question of American claims which have arisen or may arise as a result of the existing dispute between China and Japan, the Department submits for your guidance, in general, the following observations:

The history of international claims shows that the decisions made are usually based upon most careful considerations of rather fine points of law in the light of judicial deduction of fact from conflicting evidence.

What principle or principles of law will be involved in the proper determination of any claims will, almost of necessity, depend upon the evidence of facts which may be submitted by the claimants and the American diplomatic and consular officers. The all important point to be borne in mind is, therefore, that there should be collected and preserved, in the most authentic form possible, the very best evidence of all the surrounding facts in each case. The question whether there is international liability in any particular case, and, if so, whether on the part of Japan or of China, may depend upon evidence of facts concerning which it is impossible for the Department to have any definite information at this time. It is, therefore, impossible to indicate with any particularity the evidence which should be submitted in individual cases.

The fact, however, that the circumstances of the present situation at Shanghai and in other parts of China are somewhat unprecedented will probably accentuate the difficulty of reaching proper decisions as to the essential facts and controlling principles of law applicable to the resulting claims and makes the use of great care in their preparation the more necessary.

In view of the foregoing, probably the only action the Department can appropriately take at this time to assist claimants is to emphasize to the diplomatic and consular officers, and through them to the claimants, the necessity of procuring now, while the evidence is fresh, complete and accurate evidence with respect to any damage suffered by American nationals, including the all essential evidence of facts tending to show international responsibility of either Japan or China for the damage.

The protection of the rights of American citizens in future claims
does not usually depend upon the filing of protests by diplomatic and consular officers with the offending Governments at the time of the injury. An international wrong is not made a right by lack of protest against it, although protests may be helpful in preventing repetition of the wrongs.
2. Repeat the above to the. Legation with the Department’s request that it be made the basis of a circular instruction at an early date to consular officers in China.
